sections.label: A
sections.title: Multiple Choice Questions
sections.instruction: Answer all questions. Each question carries 1 mark.

questions.text: What is the probability of a sure (or certain) event?
If P(E) = 0.05, what is the probability of 'not E'?
Which of the following values cannot be the probability of an event?
One card is drawn from a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards. What is the probability of getting a king of red colour?
If a fair die is thrown once, what is the probability of getting a number greater than 4?

sections.label: B
sections.title: Short Questions
sections.instruction: Answer all questions. Each question carries 3 marks.

questions.text: Explain what is meant by an 'impossible event' and provide an illustrative example using a single throw of a die.
questions.answer: An event which is impossible to occur has a probability of 0 and is called an impossible event. For example, in a single throw of a die, the outcomes can only be 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6. Thus, getting a number 8 is an impossible event, and its probability is 0/6 = 0.

questions.text: A bag contains 3 red balls and 5 black balls. A ball is drawn at random from the bag. What is the probability that the ball drawn is (i) red? (ii) not red?
questions.answer:
Total number of balls = 3 + 5 = 8.
(i) Probability of getting a red ball = Number of red balls / Total number of balls = 3/8.
(ii) Probability of getting a ball that is not red = 1 - P(red) = 1 - 3/8 = 5/8 (or alternatively, 5/8 representing the black balls).

questions.text: Why is tossing a coin considered to be a fair way of deciding which team should get the ball at the beginning of a football game?
questions.answer: A coin is assumed to be fair (symmetrical and unbiased) and is allowed to fall freely without interference (a random toss). In this setup, there are only two possible outcomes: head and tail. Since these outcomes are equally likely, each has a probability of 1/2. Thus, the result of the toss is completely unpredictable and unbiased, making it a fair decision-making tool.

questions.text: Savita and Hamida are friends. What is the probability that both will have (i) different birthdays? (ii) the same birthday? (Ignore leap years).
questions.answer:
Total number of days in a non-leap year = 365.
(i) If Hamida's birthday is different from Savita's, the number of favorable outcomes for her birthday is 365 - 1 = 364. Thus, P(different birthdays) = 364/365.
(ii) P(same birthday) = 1 - P(different birthdays) = 1 - 364/365 = 1/365.

sections.label: C
sections.title: Numerical Problems
sections.instruction: Answer all questions. Each question carries 4 marks.

questions.text:
A box contains 5 red marbles, 8 white marbles, and 4 green marbles. One marble is taken out of the box at random. What is the probability that the marble taken out will be:
(i) red?
(ii) white?
(iii) not green?
questions.answer:
Total number of marbles in the box = 5 (red) + 8 (white) + 4 (green) = 17.
(i) P(red) = Number of red marbles / Total marbles = 5/17.
(ii) P(white) = Number of white marbles / Total marbles = 8/17.
(iii) Number of marbles that are not green = Total marbles - Green marbles = 17 - 4 = 13. Therefore, P(not green) = 13/17.

questions.text:
Two dice, one blue and one grey, are thrown at the same time. Write down the total number of possible outcomes and calculate the probability that the sum of the two numbers appearing on top of the dice is:
(i) 8
(ii) 13
(iii) less than or equal to 12
questions.answer:
The total number of possible outcomes when two dice are thrown is 6 * 6 = 36.
(i) Outcomes favorable to getting a sum of 8 are (2,6), (3,5), (4,4), (5,3), (6,2). Number of favorable outcomes = 5. Hence, P(sum is 8) = 5/36.
(ii) There is no outcome that gives a sum of 13. The number of favorable outcomes = 0. Hence, P(sum is 13) = 0.
(iii) Since the maximum possible sum is 12 (from (6,6)), all 36 outcomes have a sum less than or equal to 12. Hence, P(sum <= 12) = 36/36 = 1.

questions.text:
A piggy bank contains hundred 50p coins, fifty Rs 1 coins, twenty Rs 2 coins, and ten Rs 5 coins. If it is equally likely that one of the coins will fall out when the bank is turned upside down, what is the probability that the coin:
(i) will be a 50p coin?
(ii) will not be a Rs 5 coin?
questions.answer:
Total number of coins in the piggy bank = 100 (50p) + 50 (Rs 1) + 20 (Rs 2) + 10 (Rs 5) = 180.
(i) Number of 50p coins = 100.
P(50p coin) = 100/180 = 5/9.
(ii) Number of Rs 5 coins = 10.
Number of coins that are not Rs 5 coins = 180 - 10 = 170.
P(not a Rs 5 coin) = 170/180 = 17/18.
